title: AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder

lean_statement.name: isAcyclic_iff_hasTopologicalOrder
lean_statement.code:
/-- claim_3_2 (`AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder`): a CDMG `G` is acyclic
iff it has a topological order. Mirrors
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` Lem at line 224 verbatim,
using `CDMG.IsAcyclic` (def_3_6) on the left and the existential
`CDMG.HasTopologicalOrder` (def_3_8) on the right. The `[Finite ╬▒]`
hypothesis is needed for the (тЗТ) direction's parent-free-node
extraction; the (тЗР) direction does not use finiteness. The proof
phase can recover a concrete `Fintype ╬▒` instance from `Finite ╬▒`
via `Fintype.ofFinite` if the constructive enumeration is needed. -/
theorem isAcyclic_iff_hasTopologicalOrder
    [Finite ╬▒] (G : CDMG ╬▒) :
    G.IsAcyclic тЖФ G.HasTopologicalOrder := by

lean_explanation:
The theorem fixes a CDMG `G : CDMG ╬▒` over an ambient vertex type `╬▒ : Type*` тАФ the LN's silent "let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG" carries no built-in finiteness, so at def_3_1's level `╬▒` is fully polymorphic. The extra `[Finite ╬▒]` instance hypothesis encodes the LN's assumption that the node set $J \cup V$ is finite: without it, the (тЗТ) direction's iterative parent-free-node extraction (LN lines 227--245) does not terminate. The conclusion is the biconditional `G.IsAcyclic тЖФ G.HasTopologicalOrder`, with `IsAcyclic` (def_3_6) on the left expressing "no non-trivial directed walk closes into a cycle" and `HasTopologicalOrder` (def_3_8) on the right expressing the existential `тИГ r, IsTopologicalOrder G r` тАФ the LN's prose "$G$ has a topological order".

The one Mathlib idiom worth flagging is the choice of `[Finite ╬▒]` rather than `[Fintype ╬▒]`. `Finite` is the `Prop`-valued finiteness predicate; `Fintype` is its data-valued sibling, which additionally packages a concrete enumeration `Finset ╬▒`. Since the iff itself is `Prop`-valued and never mentions an enumeration, `Finite` is the lighter and Mathlib-linter-preferred form, and Lean's instance synthesis still lets a caller in a `[Fintype ╬▒]` context apply the theorem without supplying anything explicit (`Finite.of_fintype` fires automatically). When the proof needs a concrete enumeration internally, it recovers one on the fly via `Fintype.ofFinite`.

The shape is the standard "fix a graph, then state an equivalence of two predicates of it" pattern: a single biconditional with no further hypotheses beyond the finiteness instance. Downstream callers reach for `.mp` (acyclic тЗТ has topological order) or `.mpr` (has topological order тЗТ acyclic) to extract whichever direction they need.
design_choices:
**Existential right-hand side `G.HasTopologicalOrder`, not the relation-level `IsTopologicalOrder G r`.** The LN's Lem reads "$G$ ... has a topological order" тАФ the order is existentially quantified. `HasTopologicalOrder` (defined in `TopologicalOrder.lean`) is exactly the unwrap `тИГ r, IsTopologicalOrder G r`, so it lines up verbatim with the LN's "has". An alternative iff `G.IsAcyclic тЖФ тИА r, IsTopologicalOrder G r` would be false (the trivial relation is never a topological order of a non-empty graph), and `G.IsAcyclic тЖФ IsTopologicalOrder G r` for a *fixed* `r` would be strictly stronger than the LN's statement, which only asserts existence.

**`[Finite ╬▒]` instance hypothesis on the iff.** The LN's own proof of the (тЗТ) direction (`graphs.tex` lines 227--245) iteratively extracts a parent-free node from the induced subgraph on the still-unselected nodes, and that construction terminates only because $J \cup V$ is finite тАФ the LN says explicitly "since $G_i$ is acyclic and finite, it has a node with no parents". def_3_1's `CDMG` is polymorphic in `╬▒` and carries no built-in finiteness, so the assumption must enter as an extra hypothesis somewhere on the iff. The (тЗР) direction does not use it; we still attach the hypothesis to the iff itself rather than splitting into two lemmas (see below).

**`[Finite ╬▒]` (Prop-valued) rather than `[Fintype ╬▒]` (data-valued).** The iff is `Prop`-valued and never mentions a concrete enumeration. Mathlib's `linter.unusedFintypeInType` flags `[Fintype ╬▒]` in exactly this situation and recommends `[Finite ╬▒]`. The Prop-valued instance is the weakest finiteness hypothesis the statement needs, silences the linter, and still composes with the full `Fintype` API inside the proof (which can recover an enumeration via `Fintype.ofFinite` from `Mathlib.Data.Fintype.EquivFin` whenever it needs one). Callers writing `G.isAcyclic_iff_hasTopologicalOrder` in a `[Fintype ╬▒]` context get the `Finite ╬▒` instance for free via `Finite.of_fintype`.

**`[Finite ╬▒]` rather than an explicit `(G.J тИк G.V).Finite`.** The set-level finiteness hypothesis would be strictly weaker тАФ it leaves the ambient carrier `╬▒` potentially infinite, constraining only the node set тАФ and matches the LN's prose "finiteness of $J \cup V$" literally. It would also extend more cleanly to settings where the ambient carrier is uncountable (e.g. тДЭ-valued nodes in chapter 4 CBNs) while the chosen CDMG itself happens to have a finite node set. We default to the typeclass form for ergonomics: it carries no extra explicit argument at use sites, composes directly with Mathlib's `Finite` / `Fintype` API, and matches the iSCM chapters' default ambient-type assumption. The implication `Finite ╬▒ тЖТ (G.J тИк G.V).Finite` is one `Set.toFinite` call away, so callers in the more general regime can downshift locally if they need to.

**Single bundled iff, not two separate lemmas.** Splitting into `isAcyclic_of_hasTopologicalOrder` and `hasTopologicalOrder_of_isAcyclic` was considered, but the LN states the equivalence as one Lem; bundling matches that prose and lets `simp` / `rw` rewrite freely between the two predicates. Callers who only need one direction use `.mp` (acyclic тЗТ has topological order) or `.mpr` (has topological order тЗТ acyclic).

**Name `isAcyclic_iff_hasTopologicalOrder`, dot-projection intended; `╬▒` implicit, `[Finite ╬▒]` between, `G` explicit.** Downstream callers write `G.isAcyclic_iff_hasTopologicalOrder.mp ha`, which reads as the LN's prose "$G$ is acyclic iff $G$ has a topological order". This parallels every other claim-of-CDMG theorem in the section (`no_arrowhead_into_input`, `input_edge_target_mem_V`, `input_nodes_not_adjacent` in `JNodeProperties.lean`). The binder order тАФ implicit type, then instance argument, then explicit data тАФ is Mathlib's convention for "fix an object, state a property of it" theorems, and lets Lean's instance synthesis resolve `[Finite ╬▒]` automatically at every use site that has either a `Finite` or `Fintype` instance in scope.
